items.title: Data retention is the bit that matters for the NHS
items.body: The loudest objection to Fable 5 was a 30-day retention-with-human-review policy that applied even to customers promised zero retention, prompting Microsoft to restrict it within an hour. Under UK GDPR and the common-law duty of confidentiality that is the whole game: never put identifiable patient data into a consumer AI, and confirm any clinical tool contractually guarantees no human review and no training on your inputs.

items.title: Concentration of power, concentration of dependence
items.body: NLW's worry that one lab could gatekeep who builds the future has a quieter NHS echo: if a single vendor underpins your scribe, your letters and your triage, a policy change or price rise lands on you with no alternative. If Zitron is right that these firms are financially shaky, vendor lock-in becomes a continuity-of-care risk, not just a budget one. Keep your data portable.

items.title: Trust, once broken
items.body: The episode is a case study in how fast professional users abandon a tool over trust rather than capability. The same holds in practice: clinician confidence in an AI scribe survives on transparency about what it stores and where. Choose vendors who tell you plainly.
